Partial Data, Full Identity

DUE DILIGENCE OPERATION

The Context

My client received a project invitation; it's from an unknown person who is alleging a past relationship with the client. The problem? My client does not remember this person and doesn't have any picture of the person.

The Objective

Check if the alleged identity and organization are real; check if the target (unknown person) is already linked to the organization.

Initial data

I only have a partial name (one name, one last name), organization name, target organizational email, and phone number; is it enough to start? Yes.

Also, from the email it's possible to derive the organization web domain, and also the body of an email message sent by the target to my client to analyze the content and the intention of this approach.

Initialize the process.

I use my super powerful h4x0r tool, Google, using dorks crafted with this structure:

[input partial name] + [client city] <> "John Doe" + California

Why the client city, based on the past alleged relationship (client-target), my client never changed their location, and as a filter because the target has a rather common name.

As this is an OSINT operation, I exploit the found public sources, for example, public governmental docs. Here is a match with the target's partial name, but this record has a full name with their Mexican fiscal ID (RFC); due to the dork, the document is from the government of the used city name.

This result is not a confirmation at all but opens new possibilities about a possible full name or personal information derived from the RFC, but no more relevant information was found with this dork.

I got stuck with the previous dork, then I searched for the organization's social media account. I found an X account; one of the posts from past years has a Mercado Libre article. It shows the partial name of the seller. Is it relevant? Yes, this seller's name matches the two names found on the governmental document.

Using one of my superpowers (the most basic skill), web development, I'm working on a tool for searching Mexican citizen records (legal? sure(?)). Then I tried it here, with the fiscal identifier, now that I have a hint about the potential target's full name.

My super tool (just a CRUD) returns the citizen record with the previously found full name, the citizen identifier (CURP), birth date, and an address, but the address is from a date so far from the client location (the city used in the dork).

What about this different city? It makes sense when I find another public document using the dork company name, for example, "EVIL CROP GROUP" In this case, the document from 3 years ago is a providers list when the company is mentioned in a record with an address in the same city as the target citizen record.

Data resume:
- Potential target full name
- Citizen identifiers
- Organization social media accounts

Pivot new search structures

With the potential full name of the target at this point just a hypothesis, I used the last name of the target to search in the followers/following of the social accounts, mainly Instagram. I found some accounts; the most relevant is a woman that shares the first and last name with the target, with analysis over the account posts (structured stalking), and I got:

  • Post where the woman shows and mentions a man as their brother and twin (as a joke)
  • Some of these posts have comments from a male account (sharing the first name and the first last name with the target) mentioning the woman (account owner) and the alleged brother as his sons.

I think in a family circle hypothesis:
- The woman is the target's sister
- The man who commented is the father
- And the alleged brother is the target

Following the company traces, a LinkedIn profile was returned in a result. The name in the account is from a woman who has the same last name as the maternal last name of the target, and the experience section says she is the director of the company. Another important thing is the first name of the woman in the LinkedIn profile and the first name of the target make up the organization name, for example:

  • Target name: "Rodolfo"
  • Woman's name: "Kimberly"
  • Then the organization name is: "The Kimrod Corp."

Based on this woman's name (from LinkedIn), I searched in my tool (it works fine), and it returned a record from the client city. By the age and the last name, I thought that she is the target's mother, but I don't have a confirmation at this point.

Also, in some of the company's social media accounts, a location is shared as the business site. It's in a city mentioned in the bio descriptions of the identified Instagram accounts of the father and sister and also in the mother's LinkedIn, assuming they are his relatives, although this has not been confirmed yet. Well, in the Google Maps location, there is a review written by a user with the same name as the LinkedIn account.

Returning to the family ties, I search for the sister's information in my tool (yes, again). I use the previous information to try to recreate the real name:

[name_mentioned_on_instagram] [target_last_names]

The result? A record of a woman, whose location? The client city, the full name? so relevant because the second name is the first name of the woman that it considers the target's mother, In my tool I can share with people on the same address then. It returned me a man with different last names, maybe a husband.

In this woman's bio, she mentions that she has two sons; it's like mother of Romulo and Remo. Then I search the sons' names in their following/followers list. What do I get? their accounts, the last name? the same as the man I found with the same address, then I thought he was the father.

This familiar core hypothesis can be reinforced with the organization's social media accounts because two past posts from more than 10 years ago mention a woman as the sister of the account owner and say she is my "twin." This term of "twin" is also used by the woman on Instagram with their brother's pictures. Also, there is another post where the account owner mentions their nephews and shows the pictures of two kids; the woman on Instagram also mentions two names as their sons.

In the first account post the user have replies with another username maybe the first one, It's another form to call the people with the target's name.

Remember the man that I though is the target's father?, I found in his following list an account with the second name and the two last names of the target.

Data Resume:
- Potential family core
- Links between social accounts users and family
- Organization location site
- Potential Director

What about the organization???

I perform an infrastructure recon, Domain mapping with DNS dumpster, and also I tried to get information about the domain registry but It have allowed from the first record the privacy option, but I got two web sites one from the main domain another subdomain with a name about the project(used as approach pretext) industrie, both are google sites, and the UI looks lie full AI made, and have contact methods same phone in both 2 new organization emails.

The mapping also return IP address but, I don't need it

The creation dates from the previous identified social media accounts have more than 3 years, are no recent, it's relevant because some attackers do new accounts or sites for social Engineering campaigns, also it have real followers, and some of them based on their names look like target familiars.

Then is the company real? I Think yes, also there is public documents like the providers list mentioned, the LinkedIn account have the owner identification checked with Mexican citizen ID Card, they have organization emails, it's not a factor to discard risks because some malicious actos use organizational emails to to heir bad things, but it looks real, but I can to check if the project mention by the target in the invitation to my client is real, although the website mentions services that are in line with the project's scope.

So what about all this information?

Is the OSINT only collect information Googling like a pro?, No bro, the analysis matters, then I filter and use the information relevant to my intelligence requirements, I also search for the organizational emails in leaks and I found some ULPs, but I Don't need to know passwords, what matters about this leaks? where accounts are linked to the emails.

You Don't Know what a ULP is, check my post about it

Also I use the family core just for confirm my hypotesis about the target real identity, It really doesn't matter for the investigation whether he has a sister or nephews, but from the sister Instagram account I extract pictures about the real face of the target, needed to show to my client.

Some information just make noise other is just for verification, If the data meets Intel's requirements, it will be retained, but I'ts mean, I just filter out the useless information, and that's it?, NO, I use to document about the users auto exposures, and how a real malicious actor can exploit it, I put in the final report the relevant things to solve the requirements, and how I justify them.

But also I create my own documentation about new TTPs, or what new OPSEC practice are needed to protect the people information, simple things like:

  • Don't use your real name(full) on social accounts
  • Don't mention your relatives names
  • Don't post your kids pictures
  • Use privacy options like private profiles, it works but it's not it is not flawless
  • Don't post your job location
  • Review your past post and delete auto exposures

People don't care about your daily routine, but attackers do, all information about you can be exploited.

And remember, you can protect your self, but your family can expose your data in their post.

In resume the following image show the investigation path from the initial data to the recreation of the family core.

#YourPrivacySucks
